HewMann Experience – Texas Parent Advocacy Checklist

Source: Texas Parent Advocacy Checklist (hewmannexperienc.com)

About This Resource

HewMann Experience offers a Texas Parent Advocacy Checklist to help families prepare for ARD, IEP, and 504 conversations with more clarity and confidence. It is designed to help parents organize their thoughts, gather the

confidence. It is designed to help parents organize their thoughts, gather the right documents, and walk into school meetings ready to advocate clearly.

1. Before the Meeting

Write down your top 3 concerns — keep them specific, recent, and tied to school impact.

Review the last ARD, IEP, 504, progress report, evaluations, and teacher updates.

Make a quick timeline of what has happened: concer

ations, and teacher updates.

Make a quick timeline of what has happened: concerns raised, meetings held, emails sent, changes tried, and what still is not working.

Decide what you want from the meeting — answers, accommodations, evaluation, service changes, documentation, or a follow-up plan.

2. What to Bring with You

A folder with evaluations, report cards, work samples, behavior notes, email

ou

A folder with evaluations, report cards, work samples, behavior notes, emails, and prior meeting paperwork.

A short written parent statement describing your child’s strengths, needs, patterns, and what support would help.

A printed question list so you do not forget key points during the meeting.

A notebook or meeting notes page to capture who said what, next steps, and timelines.

3. Quest

eeting notes page to capture who said what, next steps, and timelines.

3. Questions to Ask During the Meeting

What data is the school using to support this recommendation?

How is my child performing compared with grade-level expectations and classroom peers?

What supports, accommodations, or services are available right now?

What does implementation look like in the classroom, and who is resp

right now?

What does implementation look like in the classroom, and who is responsible for each support?

How will progress be measured, documented, and shared with me?

If we try this plan, when will the team reconvene to review whether it is working?

4. If You Disagree or Need Time

Ask for the recommendation to be put in writing.

Ask for a copy of the draft plan, notes, and any evaluation da

put in writing.

Ask for a copy of the draft plan, notes, and any evaluation data discussed.

State clearly that you need time to review before agreeing if you are not ready.

Follow up by email after the meeting to summarize your understanding and preserve documentation.

5. Texas-Specific Reminders

Texas schools often use the term ARD for special education meetings. If your child receives spec

ten use the term ARD for special education meetings. If your child receives special education services, learn the ARD process and your role in it.

If your child does not qualify for special education, ask whether a Section 504 plan, campus intervention, or other support options should be considered.

Bring concerns tied to classroom access, executive functioning, behavior, communication, sensory

ed to classroom access, executive functioning, behavior, communication, sensory needs, transitions, and academic impact.

Ask for timelines, next steps, and who will follow up. Verbal reassurance is helpful, but written documentation matters.

6. Signs You May Want Extra Advocacy Support

You may benefit from additional support if: - You feel overwhelmed or unheard in school meetings. - The school

l support if: - You feel overwhelmed or unheard in school meetings. - The school team uses language you do not fully understand. - You keep getting vague answers instead of a clear plan. - You need help organizing records, preparing questions, or responding after the meeting. - Your child needs support across behavior, communication, accommodations, transitions, or repeated school concerns.

Servi

communication, accommodations, transitions, or repeated school concerns.

Services Offered

HewMann Experience offers family-centered advocacy support, including: - Meeting preparation - Record review - Strategic guidance for parents who want to walk into school conversations more organized, confident, and clear.

About HewMann Experience

HewMann Experience is a premium, private-pay, non-clinical advocacy and family support service run by Natasha Hewlett Mann. It is designed for families navigating neurodivergence, school systems, overwhelm, and complex decisions.

The busi

ing neurodivergence, school systems, overwhelm, and complex decisions.

The business is personal, professional, and premium — not generic, not clinical, and not transactional.

What HewMann Experience Offers

HewMann Experience provides non-clinical, high-touch advocacy, strategy, and family support, including:

Meeting preparation — helping parents prepare for ARD, IEP, and 504 meetings

Record r

g preparation — helping parents prepare for ARD, IEP, and 504 meetings

School communication support — helping parents document, organize questions, and respond effectively

Advocacy coaching — supporting parents bef

e questions, and respond effectively

Who HewMann Experience Helps

HewMann Experience supports parents and caregivers who are raising neurodivergent children and need trusted guidance, practical strategy, and a calmer path forward. This includes:

About the Founder – Natasha Hewlett Mann

Natasha Hewlett Mann brings lived experience, strategic advocacy, and professional training in inclusion and belonging. Her qualifications include:

DEI certification through Cornell University, with a background in inclusive practices and belonging-centered

ell University, with a background in inclusive practices and belonging-centered strategy.

Deep personal and professional understanding of both the emotional and systems side of navigating neurodivergence.

Her approach is: strategic, compassionate, empowering, polished, and grounded in belonging.

“My work is grounded in both lived experience and professional training. I bring a strong background

in both lived experience and professional training. I bring a strong background in inclusion, belonging, and systems-based support, including DEI certification through Cornell University. That perspective shapes how I serve families — with empathy, dignity, and a deep respect for what it means to help children and caregivers feel seen, supported, and better equipped to move forward.”

egivers feel seen, supported, and better equipped to move forward.”

Brand Identity

The Golden Butterfly

The butterfly is a core part of the HewMann Experience brand identity.

The butterfly represents transformation with intention. It reflects growth, resilience, and the beauty of becoming who you were always meant to be — even after challenge, change, or uncertainty. For the families and indiv

to be — even after challenge, change, or uncertainty. For the families and individuals served, it symbolizes support through transition, finding your voice, and moving from overwhelm to empowerment. Personally for Natasha, it represents grace, strength, and the courage to evolve while still honoring who you are at your core.

The gold color reflects that transformation is not only beautiful, but v

core.

The gold color reflects that transformation is not only beautiful, but valuable. Gold symbolizes excellence, warmth, worth, and refinement. It reflects the premium, intentional, and empowering experience of the brand. The gold butterfly represents rising through challenge and emerging with clarity, confidence, and purpose.

Scope of Service – Disclaimer

Services are designed to help parents and caregivers better understand options, prepare for meetings, communicate more effectively, and navigate school and family systems with greater clarity.

What is the difference between a 504 Plan and an IEP?
An IEP includes specialized instruction and services. A 504 Plan provides accommodation

includes specialized instruction and services. A 504 Plan provides accommodations only.
How do I know if my child qualifies?
If a disability impacts learning, your child may qualify. A school evaluation determines this.
What happens during an IEP/ARD meeting?
The team reviews needs, sets goals, and agrees on supports and services.
What is an ARD meeting?
In Texas, ARD is simply the official name f

nd services.
What is an ARD meeting?
In Texas, ARD is simply the official name for an IEP meeting.
Can a child have both?
No—students typically have either an IEP or a 504 Plan.
What if the school says no?
You can request reevaluation, an independent evaluation, or challenge the decision.
What is coaching?
It focuses on building real-life skills like organization, planning, and follow-through.
How

n building real-life skills like organization, planning, and follow-through.
How is it different from therapy?
Coaching builds systems and skills; therapy treats mental health conditions.
What does neurodiversity-affirming mean?
It respects how your child’s brain works and builds on strengths instead of forcing change.
Who do you work with?
Children, teens, and adults.
